title: Good job, but extremely stressful
pros: Apple does really focus on a work life balance and allows you to take a lot of time to yourself if needed (and approved).
cons: Complete and total burnout is expected. Data Center logistics can be good, but expect a LOT to be put on your plate. I cannot count the amount of projects that we (or just me) would be handed and told next to nothing about how to accomplish it. It was an extremely, "sink or swim" place that, I think, THEY thought made you stronger, but in reality it just makes you question why you're going at 150% at all times for the salary you are making.

title: Make a Difference for our Senior Community- Great Benefits- High Stess
body: Make a Difference for our Senior Community- Great Benefits- High Stess
pros: A Culture of Connection and Innovation Apple provides an environment where the "human element" is truly prioritized in the customer journey. The commitment to Steps of Service allows Specialists to go beyond simple transactions and build genuine rapport. Additionally, the peer-to-peer support is exceptional; working alongside highly skilled Technicians and fellow Specialists creates a collaborative atmosphere where everyone is trying to deliver excellence despite operational hurdles. It is a fast-paced, high-energy environment that rewards those who thrive on problem-solving and authentic connection.
cons: Operational Strains and Leadership Visibility Gaps The primary challenge lies in the disconnect between "peak volume" and "leadership presence." During high-traffic periods, there is often a lack of visible management on the floor, leaving Specialists and Leads to navigate escalations, overrides, and sales guidance without adequate support. This often results in "vague promises" to customers (e.g., "someone will be with you momentarily") rather than actual service. When managers remain in back-office admin roles during these critical windows, it places an immense burden on the frontline team, leading to burnout and a feeling of being "stretched beyond capacity."

title: Great Benefits, Good place to work depending on dept
body: Great Benefits, Good place to work depending on dept
pros: Apple is still an incredible company with world-class talent, strong benefits, and the opportunity to work on products and systems that operate at massive global scale. The people on the groundâengineers, operators, program managersâare deeply committed, smart, and mission-driven. When teams are empowered, the impact can be incredibly rewarding.
cons: Within IS&T the recent wave of reorganizations has been deeply discouraging. Roles and responsibilities have shifted frequently, often without clear strategy or long-term vision. Strong contributors have been moved into positions they did not want or were never designed for, while leadership promotions increasingly appear disconnected from operational experience or proven impact. There is also a growing sense of cultural narrowing at the leadership level. Decision-making and advancement seem to favor a very specific leadership profile and way of thinking, which limits diversity of perspective and can stifle healthy debate and innovation. As a result, many experienced employees feel stuck, undervalued, or uncertain about their future despite years of strong performance.

title: Innovative environment with high impact, but can feel siloed
body: Innovative environment with high impact, but can feel siloed
pros: Compensation and benefits are truly top-tier, with great stock grants and health insurance. Work on products that impact millions of people worldwide. Colleagues are generally brilliant and passionate, fostering a strong engineering culture. Access to cutting-edge technology and resources is unparalleled. The company's focus on user privacy and security is commendable and a genuine point of pride.
cons: Extremely compartmentalized structure; you often feel like a small cog in a vast machine with limited visibility. Internal processes can be slow and bureaucratic, hindering rapid innovation. Work-life balance varies wildly by team, with some groups having intense crunch periods. A culture of secrecy can stifle open collaboration and make cross-team projects challenging. Career growth sometimes feels more dependent on politics and visibility than pure technical merit.

title: It was great until it wasn't
body: It was great until it wasn't
pros: Great to get your feet wet in "tech" very retail based now. The relationships I was able to foster at Apple will last a life time. I had to privilege to make a lot of great friends. I also was able to develop key skills that will help me excel in an enterprise environment. It also forced me to communicate with a wide range of people which helped with my communication skills greatly,
cons: No work life balance. I worked every weekend after I became a genius making it feel impossible to see any of my family or friends. The managers were quite poor with bad decision making skills and showed clear favoritism. They moved my old Store Manager to a different store after word spread around that we were planing on unionizing. The new Store Manager was the worst person I've worked for. Extremely cut throat in every way possible. He would smile in your face and tell you how great of a job your doing turn around and fire you the next hour about some random "policy" violation. I was skipped over for multiple promotions even after showing that I was willing and ready. Leaving this job was the best decision I've ever made. I would never work at another fruit stand again.

title: Great experience, but not what it used to be
body: Great experience, but not what it used to be
pros: Incredible opportunity to work on the world's best products and most of the people really care about the product. It was a lot of fun to see how the world's most admired company works behind the scenes. If you have a good manager, you can go pretty far, pretty quick. Compensation and PTO is very fair for Silicon Valley. The culture is very design and user experience driven which may seem obvious, but you should feel the same way before joining. Sometimes sacrifices in the technical implementation are made because it provides a better user design.
cons: My main complaint is that it gets repetitive after a few years, specifically working on the OS side, you just feel like it's the same cycle-driven work year in and year out. Work life balance can be tough, especially leading up to major OS releases. The hybrid schedule is nice, but the in office days force you to commute to/from Cupertino to sit in online meetings. I put a low score for DEI because there is not a huge representation of underserved minorities in tech. That becomes apparent from day one on campus, despite what the values are of the company.

pros: Pros Brand prestige: Working at Apple instantly boosts your rÃ©sumÃ©. It opens doors in tech, marketing, design, and even unrelated industries. Culture of excellence: Youâre surrounded by sharp, detail-obsessed people. The standards are sky-high, which can push you to grow faster than anywhere else. Training and structure: Whether youâre in a store or corporate, training is thorough. Appleâs onboarding and communication systems are elite. Diversity and inclusion (mostly real): Company-wide emphasis on diversity, though it varies by location or team. Cool factor: People light up when you say you work at Apple. Thereâs real social capital attached to the brand.

title: Lots of potential, but stuck in the past
body: Lots of potential, but stuck in the past
pros: Excellent offices, good cafe, good pay, decent work life balance, interesting problems to solve, if you can get to do them.
cons: Extreme (and largely useless) secrecy makes it difficult to get anything done. The "Apple way" of doing things is by plucking some people from separate teams that specialize in something and sort of mushing them together as a virtual team. This has its advantages and disadvantages. Advantage: those teams can really deeply specialize in a narrow sub-field, and as such deliver insights and results that generalists can't deliver. You can see this in Apple products every day. Disadvantage: this works only on the hardware side. On the software side, I would much rather have teams of capable generalists working on products, but with the ability (and requirement) to reach out to specialists when needed. That'd work way better than "Apple way" works today.
role: Former employee - Senior Staff Software Engineer

pros: Amazing culture, benefits, people, and brand to be apart of it. If you like retail (but not commission) you can make great money at Apple.
cons: Yearly raises are average. Retail hours are challenging. It's hard to join corporate if you work in the stores.

title: Waste of Years
pros: As someone who worked for Apple for almost a decade in roles ranging from retail to corporate, the pros have drastically reduced for existing tenured people. For new workers, potential pros: -decent health insurance -education opportunities although heavily stipulated

title: Great Experience as a Campus Rep â Fun Job and Good Pay
body: Great Experience as a Campus Rep â Fun Job and Good Pay
pros: Working as a Campus Rep for Apple was a really enjoyable experience. The role gave me the chance to represent a brand I genuinely admire, connect with students, and get creative with marketing initiatives. The pay was solid for a student position, and the flexibility made it easy to balance with classes. It also looked great on my resume and helped me build professional skills in communication, event planning, and brand representation.
cons: Itâs a part-time, temporary role, so there isnât much room for long-term growth within Apple unless you move into other positions after graduation.

title: Best Part-Time Job Ever
pros: As an undergrad student, working at Apple is probably the best part-time job you could get. Aside from internships and such, working at Apple is an AMAZING experience. They hire seasonals to help during peak times throughout the year, which is how I started. You are treated fairly just as any other Apple employee even though its a retail position. cons: Sometimes support from leadership can look like micro managing. There are KPIs and although itâs not mandatory to meet them itâs highly preferred that you do and a lot of the coaching is helping you meet those kpis. Not a bad thing, although some team members will work outside of a team mindset to achieve those goals. title: Not what it used to be, but you'll definitely grow
body: Not what it used to be, but you'll definitely grow
pros: Great benefits, incredible experiences, you can purse many interests and learn a lot. Depending on where you enter the company (and your leadership team) it can truly change your life.
cons: Most entry-level or mid-level people should realistically expect a maximum of 2 significant raises/promotions across your time with the company unless you exit and come back in a higher role (called a boomerang). The pace of work can be very manic, especially if you are in a global facing role. Expect to find yourself investing heavily in work that will either never materialize as a complete end-product or be compromised significantly to the point that it doesn't deliver the promised ROI. Not long ago Apple still had tons of start-up level energy but now has taken on the same political infighting and administrative burden that handicap most well-intentioned technology companies.

title: Abysmal at AppleCare
pros: Offers standard health benefits (of which you pay for), 401K w/ 6% match, and a variety of other benefits that you may or may not utilize. Nothing outstanding, but solid, standard offerings. The campuses are nice with nice amenities such as a gym, coffee & juice bars (that you pay for), walking trails, and generally well kept internal and external spaces to sit. The caffÃ¨ has good food at a discount if compared to a restaurant serving the same thing, for example. There are snack machines. There is free cheap coffee and water. There are medical offices and a pharmacy on campus. 99% of my experiences with other employees have been drama free. In other words, Apple tends to hire well mannered, professional individuals who have their stuff together rather than people who create drama out of nothing, or that are unstable, etc. You get discounts on both Apple and select third party products and services.

title: ZERO work-life balance, pay is low, pressure is insane
body: ZERO work-life balance, pay is low, pressure is insane
pros: Company wide policy of flexible/hybrid work Mondays and Fridays, good health plan coverage with low out of pocket maximums (approximately 2k for HDHP). Apparently some teams are better than others for work-life balance. HWTE sucks.
cons: Work-life balance is nonexistent, pay is poor compared to other tech companies, pressure from mgmt and subsequent turnover is extremely high. Expectations are ridiculous compared to compensation. Apple is the worst major US employer for turnover, even worse than Tesla. They also didn't plan for the amount of employees they have hired, so at most offices, including Apple Park HQ, there isn't enough parking, desk space, cafeteria tables, etc. I feel like I'm at Walmart on Boxing Day every day I come in to work.
